fuelReason: Most suburban Florida homes have natural gas lines. Propane is common in rural areas and the Keys.
recentEvent: Hurricane Helene (2024) left over 1.2 million Florida customers without power, some for more than a week.
localCode: Florida Building Code requires generators in coastal high-wind zones to be rated for 150+ mph winds. Flood zone properties require elevated mounting or waterproof enclosures. Setback requirements vary by county.
costExplanation: Florida's generator market is the largest in the US, which keeps prices competitive despite high demand. Installation costs are average nationally because the large number of certified installers creates healthy competition.
bestTimeToBuy: January through April, well before hurricane season starts June 1. Installers book up by May, and lead times double once tropical activity begins.
description: Florida homeowners face an average of 3.5 power outages per year, primarily from hurricanes and tropical storms. With summer temperatures regularly exceeding 95°F, losing air conditioning during an outage isn't just uncomfortable — it can be dangerous.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need hurricane-rated mounting for my generator in Florida?
faqSpecific.a: In coastal high-wind zones (most of South Florida, the Gulf Coast, and the Keys), yes. The Florida Building Code requires equipment rated for 150+ mph winds. Expect $300-$500 in additional mounting hardware.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator do I need for a typical Florida home?
faqSpecific.a: The average Florida home is about 1,800 sq ft. For whole-house coverage including central AC, a 20-22 kW standby generator is the standard recommendation. If you have a pool pump, go 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How long does generator permitting take in Florida?
faqSpecific.a: Typically 1-2 weeks. Some South Florida counties (Miami-Dade, Broward) can take up to 3 weeks during peak season.

faqSpecific.q: Should I choose natural gas or propane in Florida?
faqSpecific.a: If you have a natural gas line (most suburban areas), use it — unlimited fuel supply during an outage. In rural areas or the Keys, propane with a 500-gallon tank gives you 7-10 days of backup.

faqSpecific.q: Will my Florida homeowner's insurance cover a generator?
faqSpecific.a: The generator is typically covered as a permanently installed home improvement. Some Florida insurers offer 1-5% premium discounts for homes with standby generators.

fuelReason: Texas has extensive, cheap natural gas infrastructure.
recentEvent: Winter Storm Uri (2021) left 4.5 million Texas homes without power for up to 5 days in sub-freezing temperatures.
localCode: Texas follows the NEC with local amendments. ERCOT's independent grid means Texas can't import power from other states during emergencies.
costExplanation: Lower labor rates, minimal code complexity, and strong installer competition keep Texas prices below average.
bestTimeToBuy: September through February. Texas needs backup power for both summer heat AND winter freezes.
description: After Winter Storm Uri left millions without power for days in 2021, Texas homeowners know that backup power isn't optional. The ERCOT grid operates independently from the rest of the US, meaning Texas can't import power during emergencies.

faqSpecific.q: Will my generator work during a winter storm like Uri?
faqSpecific.a: Yes. A standby generator with natural gas runs independently of the grid. During Uri, homes with standby generators kept heat, lights, and running water while neighbors didn't.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator do I need for a Texas home?
faqSpecific.a: Average Texas home is about 2,100 sq ft. For whole-house coverage including AC, a 22 kW unit is standard. Large homes with pools should look at 24-26 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Texas?
faqSpecific.a: 1-3 weeks depending on the municipality. Houston and Dallas are on the longer end.

faqSpecific.q: Is the Texas grid more reliable now than during Uri?
faqSpecific.a: ERCOT has made improvements, but the fundamental vulnerability remains — Texas can't import power from other states. A standby generator is the only guaranteed backup.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane for a Texas generator?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas for most homes. Texas has abundant, cheap natural gas. Propane for rural properties without gas service.

fuelReason: Natural gas is widely available and cheap. Propane is common in rural parishes.
recentEvent: Hurricane Ida (2021) knocked out power to all of New Orleans for over two weeks. Parts of southeast Louisiana were without electricity for six weeks.
localCode: Louisiana follows the NEC with state amendments. Flood zone installations require elevated mounting. New Orleans has specific permit requirements.
costExplanation: Below-average costs due to lower cost of living and labor rates. Flood zone requirements add $200-$600 for elevated mounting in many parishes.
bestTimeToBuy: January through April. After Ida, installer wait times exceeded 6 months. Buy early.
description: Louisiana experiences some of the longest power outages in the nation, averaging over 13 hours per interruption. Entergy's aging infrastructure means even tropical storms cause multi-day outages.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need flood-rated generator mounting in Louisiana?
faqSpecific.a: In designated flood zones (common across coastal and low-lying parishes), yes. The generator must be mounted above the base flood elevation. This adds $200-$600 to installation cost.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Louisiana home?
faqSpecific.a: Average Louisiana home is about 1,700 sq ft. A 20 kW generator covers whole-house including AC. Don't go essential-only unless you're fine sweating for a week.

faqSpecific.q: How long will I wait for installation in Louisiana?
faqSpecific.a: Off-season: 2-3 weeks. During hurricane season: 4-8 weeks. After a major storm: 3-6 months. Post-Ida, some waited until 2022.

faqSpecific.q: Is Entergy's grid getting more reliable?
faqSpecific.a: Entergy has invested since Ida, but Louisiana's grid remains one of the least reliable in the nation. A standby generator is the only dependable backup.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Louisiana?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in urban areas (New Orleans, Baton Rouge, Shreveport). Propane in rural parishes. If on propane, get a 500-gallon tank minimum.

fuelReason: Natural gas in metro areas. Propane in rural Lowcountry and Upstate.
recentEvent: South Carolina recorded 53 hours of power outages in 2024 — the highest of any state. Hurricane Helene left over 1.2 million customers without power.
localCode: SC follows the IRC with state amendments. Coastal counties have wind-speed design requirements for outdoor equipment.
costExplanation: Close to national average. Coastal counties cost more due to wind-rated mounting. Upstate and Midlands are slightly below average.
bestTimeToBuy: January through April. Helene in 2024 was a wake-up call for the entire state.
description: South Carolina recorded nearly 53 hours of power outages in 2024 — the highest of any state. Hurricane Helene alone left over 1.2 million customers without power.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need wind-rated generator mounting in coastal South Carolina?
faqSpecific.a: In Charleston, Horry (Myrtle Beach), and Beaufort (Hilton Head) counties, yes. This adds $200-$500 for rated mounting hardware.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a South Carolina home?
faqSpecific.a: Average SC home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 20-22 kW unit covers whole-house. Upstate homes with electric heat may need 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in South Carolina?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most counties. Charleston can take up to 3 weeks during busy season.

faqSpecific.q: Is South Carolina really the worst state for outages?
faqSpecific.a: In 2024, yes — 53 hours of average outage time, driven largely by Hurricane Helene. The trend is getting worse.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in South Carolina?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Charleston, Columbia, Greenville metros. Propane in the rural Lowcountry and Pee Dee region.

fuelReason: Natural gas in Piedmont and metro areas. Propane in mountains and coast.
recentEvent: Hurricane Helene (2024) caused catastrophic flooding in western North Carolina, leaving communities without power for weeks.
localCode: NC State Building Code based on the IRC. Coastal counties have wind-speed requirements. Mountain counties may need elevated installations.
costExplanation: Close to national average. Research Triangle and Charlotte slightly above average. Rural areas below average.
bestTimeToBuy: January through April for coast. September through November for mountains.
description: North Carolina faces a dual threat: hurricanes along the coast and ice storms in the Piedmont and mountains. Hurricane Helene's devastation of western NC in 2024 showed that generator preparedness isn't just a coastal issue.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need special mounting on the NC coast or mountains?
faqSpecific.a: Coastal counties require wind-rated mounting. Mountain counties in flood-prone valleys may need elevated installations.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a North Carolina home?
faqSpecific.a: Average NC home is about 2,200 sq ft. A 22 kW unit is most common. Homes with heat pumps may need 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in North Carolina?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most counties. Wake (Raleigh) and Mecklenburg (Charlotte) are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: Are mountain NC homes at risk too?
faqSpecific.a: Absolutely. Helene (2024) proved western NC is vulnerable to catastrophic flooding and extended outages. Ice storms are annual threats.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in North Carolina?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Charlotte, Raleigh-Durham, Greensboro metros. Propane in mountains and many coastal communities.

fuelReason: Deregulated gas market with competitive pricing. Most metro Atlanta homes have gas.
recentEvent: Hurricane Helene (2024) pushed inland through Georgia, causing widespread outages in metro Atlanta and north Georgia.
localCode: Georgia follows the IRC. No unusual requirements. Atlanta's dense tree canopy creates unique wind/tree-fall risks.
costExplanation: Below-average costs thanks to moderate labor rates and straightforward permitting. Growing market with healthy installer competition.
bestTimeToBuy: November through March. Storm season peaks March through September.
description: Georgia's mix of severe thunderstorms, tornadoes, and Gulf Coast hurricane remnants creates persistent outage risk. Metro Atlanta's aging tree canopy makes the grid especially vulnerable.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for an Atlanta-area home?
faqSpecific.a: Average metro Atlanta home is about 2,100 sq ft. A 22 kW unit is standard. Most Atlanta homes have gas heat and electric AC.

faqSpecific.q: How does Atlanta's tree canopy affect generators?
faqSpecific.a: Atlanta has one of the densest urban tree canopies in the US. Your installer needs to place the unit away from large trees. Falling trees are the #1 cause of Atlanta outages.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Georgia?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most counties. Fulton (Atlanta) and Cobb are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: Is Georgia at risk from hurricanes?
faqSpecific.a: Coastal Georgia (Savannah) absolutely. Helene in 2024 proved metro Atlanta and north Georgia can get hit too.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Georgia?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in metro Atlanta and suburbs (deregulated, competitive pricing). Propane for rural areas and some coastal communities.

fuelReason: Natural gas via SoCalGas or PG&E. Some newer all-electric homes require propane tanks.
recentEvent: PG&E's Public Safety Power Shutoffs (PSPS) have affected millions since 2019, cutting power for 2-5 days during high fire-risk conditions.
localCode: Seismic bracing required (CBC Section 1613). Title 24 energy compliance. Fire department setback approvals in wildfire zones. Some cities have noise ordinances.
costExplanation: Most expensive state. Seismic bracing adds $800-$1,500. Strict permitting adds more. Labor rates are 40-60% above national average. A 22 kW install that costs $11,000 in Georgia costs $17,000+ here.
bestTimeToBuy: January through May. PSPS events typically begin September through November.
description: California homeowners face Public Safety Power Shutoffs (PSPS). Utilities cut power during high fire-risk conditions, leaving homes dark for days. Installations are more expensive due to seismic bracing, strict permitting, and higher labor costs.

faqSpecific.q: Does my generator need seismic bracing in California?
faqSpecific.a: Yes. CBC Section 1613 requires it. Adds $800-$1,500. Non-negotiable — your permit won't pass without it.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a California home?
faqSpecific.a: Average is about 1,800 sq ft. A 20-22 kW covers whole-house. Central Valley and desert areas need AC coverage during PSPS events.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in California?
faqSpecific.a: 2-4 weeks minimum. LA, SF, and wildfire zones can take 4-6 weeks. Slowest permitting in the country.

faqSpecific.q: Why are generators so expensive in California?
faqSpecific.a: Seismic bracing ($800-$1,500), complex permitting ($200-$500), and labor rates 40-60% above average.

faqSpecific.q: Can I use a generator during a PSPS event?
faqSpecific.a: That's the whole point. Your standby generator kicks in automatically when the utility shuts off power. PSPS events last 2-5 days typically.

fuelReason: Cheap and widely available via DTE and Consumers Energy.
recentEvent: The August 2023 derecho knocked out power to over 1 million Michigan customers.
localCode: Michigan Residential Code based on the IRC. Standard electrical permit required. No unusual state-specific requirements.
costExplanation: Below-average costs thanks to Midwest labor rates, straightforward permitting, and cheap natural gas.
bestTimeToBuy: May through August. Outages peak during winter ice storms and summer thunderstorms.
description: Michigan's harsh winters bring ice storms and heavy snow that frequently down power lines. DTE Energy customers experience some of the highest outage rates of any major US utility.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Michigan home with gas heat?
faqSpecific.a: With a gas furnace (most MI homes), a 20-22 kW generator is plenty — the blower only draws 800-1,300W. Electric heat needs 24+ kW.

faqSpecific.q: Is my sump pump covered by the generator?
faqSpecific.a: Yes, if you size for it (800-1,500W). For Michigan homes with basements, the sump pump should be on your essential circuits list.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Michigan?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most municipalities. Oakland, Wayne, and Kent counties are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: How reliable is DTE Energy?
faqSpecific.a: DTE consistently ranks among the worst major US utilities for outages. DTE customers should consider a standby generator a near-necessity.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Michigan?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas, almost always. Propane only in remote areas of the UP or northern Lower Michigan.

fuelReason: Natural gas via ConEd, National Grid, or NYSEG. Some areas have gas moratoriums requiring propane.
recentEvent: Superstorm Sandy (2012) left 2 million customers without power. Tropical Storm Isaias (2020) caused multi-day outages across Long Island.
localCode: NYS Uniform Fire Prevention and Building Code. NYC requires DOB permits with noise compliance. Long Island has wind-speed requirements.
costExplanation: Second most expensive after California. NYC labor rates are 30-50% above average. DOB permits add complexity. Long Island and Westchester are slightly less than the five boroughs.
bestTimeToBuy: December through March. Nor'easter season peaks January-March.
description: From Superstorm Sandy to annual nor'easters, New York homeowners know power outages are a matter of when, not if. Installation costs are higher in the tri-state area.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need a NYC DOB permit for generator installation?
faqSpecific.a: In the five boroughs, yes. DOB work permit required for electrical and potentially plumbing work. Takes 3-6 weeks. Outside NYC, standard municipal permits.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a New York home?
faqSpecific.a: Average suburban NY home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 22 kW is standard. NYC townhouses may get away with 16-20 kW. Larger Westchester properties need 24-26 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in New York?
faqSpecific.a: Outside NYC: 2-3 weeks. NYC DOB: 3-6 weeks. Long Island: 2-4 weeks.

faqSpecific.q: Why is installation so expensive in the NY metro area?
faqSpecific.a: Labor rates 30-50% above average, DOB permits add $500+, space constraints make logistics harder.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in New York?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in most suburbs. But several counties have gas moratoriums (National Grid territory) — check before assuming gas is available.

fuelReason: Cheap and widely available. Most Nashville and Memphis homes have gas service.
recentEvent: The March 2020 tornado outbreak destroyed neighborhoods across Nashville, causing extended power outages.
localCode: Tennessee follows the IRC with state amendments. Standard electrical permit required. No unusual requirements.
costExplanation: Below-average costs. Low labor rates, simple permitting, and TVA's reliable wholesale power keep the market competitive.
bestTimeToBuy: November through February. Tornado season runs March through June.
description: Tennessee sits in Dixie Alley — one of the most tornado-prone regions in the country. Nashville's 2020 tornado drove demand for home generators to an all-time high.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Tennessee home?
faqSpecific.a: Average is about 2,000 sq ft. A 20-22 kW handles whole-house including AC. Most TN homes have gas heat.

faqSpecific.q: Is Tennessee really in Tornado Alley?
faqSpecific.a: Dixie Alley, which has become more active than the Great Plains. Tennessee averages 15-25 tornadoes per year.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Tennessee?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most counties. Nashville and Memphis are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: Does TVA make Tennessee power more reliable?
faqSpecific.a: TVA provides reliable wholesale power, but local distribution is where outages happen. Trees and storms down local lines.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Tennessee?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Nashville, Memphis, Knoxville, Chattanooga. Propane in rural East Tennessee and the Cumberland Plateau.

fuelReason: Natural gas is widely available in Birmingham, Huntsville, and Montgomery metros. Propane is common in rural areas and the Gulf Coast.
recentEvent: Hurricane Sally (2020) caused $7.3 billion in damage across coastal Alabama, leaving hundreds of thousands without power for up to two weeks.
localCode: Alabama follows the IRC with state amendments. Coastal counties (Mobile, Baldwin) have wind-speed design requirements. No unusual statewide requirements.
costExplanation: Below-average costs driven by low labor rates and straightforward permitting. Coastal counties cost slightly more due to wind-rated hardware requirements.
bestTimeToBuy: October through February. Tornado season runs March through May, and hurricane season June through November.
description: Alabama sits squarely in Dixie Alley — one of the most tornado-active regions in the US. The Gulf Coast faces hurricane threats while the northern half endures severe thunderstorms, derechos, and ice storms. Alabama Power serves most of the state, and extended outages are common after major storm events.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for an Alabama home?
faqSpecific.a: Average Alabama home is about 1,900 sq ft. A 20-22 kW unit covers whole-house including central AC. Most homes have gas heat.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need wind-rated mounting on the Gulf Coast?
faqSpecific.a: In Mobile and Baldwin counties, yes. Wind-rated mounting hardware adds $200-$400 to the installation.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Alabama?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks statewide. Birmingham and Huntsville are the fastest.

faqSpecific.q: Is Alabama really in Tornado Alley?
faqSpecific.a: Dixie Alley. Alabama averages 40-60 tornadoes per year, more than most Great Plains states. The April 2011 super outbreak was the most destructive in US history.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Alabama?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Birmingham, Huntsville, Montgomery, and Tuscaloosa. Propane in rural areas and along the Gulf Coast.

fuelReason: Natural gas available in Jackson, Hattiesburg, and Gulf Coast metros. Propane common in rural areas across the state.
recentEvent: Hurricane Ida (2021) ripped through Mississippi before devastating Louisiana, leaving hundreds of thousands without power across the state.
localCode: Mississippi follows the IRC. Coastal counties (Harrison, Hancock, Jackson) have hurricane wind-speed requirements. Flood zone installations need elevated mounting.
costExplanation: Among the cheapest states for generator installation due to the lowest labor rates in the nation and minimal permitting complexity.
bestTimeToBuy: October through February. Hurricane season and tornado season overlap from March through November.
description: Mississippi faces a double threat from Gulf hurricanes along the coast and tornadoes inland. The state's aging grid infrastructure leads to some of the longest outage durations in the nation. Low installation costs make standby generators accessible to more homeowners here than in most states.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Mississippi home?
faqSpecific.a: Average MS home is about 1,700 sq ft. A 20 kW unit handles whole-house. Essential-only setups (10-14 kW) are popular due to lower incomes.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need flood-rated mounting in Mississippi?
faqSpecific.a: In FEMA flood zones (common along the coast and river corridors), yes. Elevated mounting adds $200-$500.

faqSpecific.q: How affordable is a generator in Mississippi?
faqSpecific.a: Mississippi has the lowest installation costs in the US. A whole-house 22 kW system starts around $7,000 installed — $2,000-$4,000 less than the national average.

faqSpecific.q: Is the Mississippi coast at hurricane risk?
faqSpecific.a: Absolutely. Hurricane Katrina devastated the Mississippi coast in 2005. Gulfport, Biloxi, and Pass Christian are in one of the highest hurricane-risk zones in the US.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Mississippi?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Jackson and larger cities. Propane is dominant in rural areas, which make up most of the state.

fuelReason: Natural gas via Dominion Energy or Washington Gas in Northern Virginia. Propane in Shenandoah Valley and rural areas.
recentEvent: Hurricane Helene (2024) and prior nor'easters have left hundreds of thousands of Virginians without power, particularly in coastal Hampton Roads and mountainous western regions.
localCode: Virginia Uniform Statewide Building Code (USBC). Coastal zone wind-speed requirements in Hampton Roads. Northern Virginia follows the USBC with county-level amendments.
costExplanation: Above-average costs driven by Northern Virginia's high labor rates (DC metro). Hampton Roads and the rest of the state are closer to the national average.
bestTimeToBuy: December through March. Hurricane season runs June through November.
description: Virginia faces hurricanes and nor'easters on the coast, ice storms in the mountains, and severe thunderstorms statewide. Northern Virginia's proximity to DC means higher labor costs but also strong installer availability.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Virginia home?
faqSpecific.a: Average VA home is about 2,100 sq ft. A 22 kW unit is standard. Northern Virginia homes with higher square footage may need 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: Is Northern Virginia more expensive for installation?
faqSpecific.a: Yes. NoVA labor rates are 20-30% above the rest of the state due to DC metro costs. Expect $11,000-$17,000 for a whole-house install in Fairfax or Arlington.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Virginia?
faqSpecific.a: 1-3 weeks. Fairfax County and Virginia Beach are on the longer end.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need wind-rated mounting in Hampton Roads?
faqSpecific.a: In Virginia Beach, Norfolk, and Chesapeake, wind-speed requirements apply. Adds $200-$400 in hardware.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Virginia?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in NoVA, Richmond, and Hampton Roads metros. Propane in the Shenandoah Valley, Blue Ridge, and rural Southside.

fuelReason: Abundant and cheap natural gas — Pennsylvania is the second-largest gas-producing state in the US. Most suburban homes have gas service.
recentEvent: The February 2024 ice storm left over 400,000 Pennsylvania customers without power. Winter storms regularly bring multi-day outages, especially in rural areas.
localCode: Pennsylvania Uniform Construction Code (UCC). Standard electrical permits required. No unusual statewide requirements. Philadelphia has additional city-level permits.
costExplanation: Near the national average. Philadelphia metro is slightly above average due to urban labor rates. Western PA and rural areas are below average.
bestTimeToBuy: May through September. Winter storms peak November through March.
description: Pennsylvania's heavy tree canopy and rural distribution lines make the grid vulnerable to ice storms and high winds. PECO and PPL serve millions across the state, and winter outages lasting multiple days are not uncommon.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Pennsylvania home?
faqSpecific.a: Average PA home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 22 kW unit covers whole-house. Homes with electric heat should go 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How does Pennsylvania's cheap natural gas help?
faqSpecific.a: PA is the second-largest gas producer in the US. Natural gas is cheap and abundant, making gas-powered generators the clear choice for most homes.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Pennsylvania?
faqSpecific.a: 1-3 weeks. Philadelphia and some suburbs take longer. Rural townships are often faster.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need a generator for ice storms?
faqSpecific.a: Ice storms are the #1 cause of extended outages in PA. A single heavy ice storm can take down power for 3-7 days in rural areas.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Pennsylvania?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Philly, Pittsburgh, and most suburbs. Propane in the Poconos, rural central PA, and mountainous areas.

fuelReason: Natural gas is cheap and widely available via Columbia Gas and Dominion Energy Ohio. Most Ohio homes have gas heat.
recentEvent: The June 2022 derecho hit central Ohio with 90+ mph winds, knocking out power to over 300,000 AEP Ohio customers for up to a week.
localCode: Ohio follows the NEC with state amendments. Standard residential electrical permit required. No unusual statewide requirements.
costExplanation: Below-average costs due to Midwest labor rates, easy permitting, and cheap natural gas. Columbus and Cincinnati are slightly above rural Ohio.
bestTimeToBuy: August through November. Tornado and storm season peaks April through July.
description: Ohio's position in the Midwest makes it vulnerable to tornadoes in spring and summer, derechos, and ice storms in winter. AEP Ohio and FirstEnergy serve the majority of the state's 5 million homes, and both utilities face ongoing reliability challenges.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for an Ohio home?
faqSpecific.a: Average Ohio home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 20-22 kW unit is standard. Most Ohio homes have gas furnaces, which makes sizing easier.

faqSpecific.q: Is Ohio tornado risk that serious?
faqSpecific.a: Ohio averages 15-20 tornadoes per year. The Memorial Day 2019 tornado outbreak hit the Dayton area with an EF4, one of the strongest ever recorded in Ohio.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Ohio?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks statewide. Columbus, Cleveland, and Cincinnati are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: How reliable is Ohio's grid?
faqSpecific.a: AEP Ohio and FirstEnergy have faced criticism for slow storm restoration. Generator ownership has increased significantly since the 2022 derecho.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Ohio?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas for nearly all Ohio homes. Propane only in remote Appalachian areas and some rural northwest Ohio communities.

fuelReason: Natural gas is only available in southern Maine (Portland area). Most of the state relies on propane, heating oil, or diesel.
recentEvent: The December 2023 nor'easter left over 400,000 CMP customers without power, with some rural areas dark for a week.
localCode: Maine Uniform Building and Energy Code (MUBEC). Standard electrical permit. No unusual requirements beyond standard NEC compliance.
costExplanation: Near the national average despite higher shipping costs to rural areas. Propane installations can cost $500-$1,000 more than natural gas due to tank requirements.
bestTimeToBuy: May through September. Nor'easter and ice storm season peaks October through March.
description: Maine experiences some of the worst winter storms on the East Coast. Central Maine Power (CMP) has been ranked among the least reliable utilities in New England, and rural areas can wait a week or more for power restoration after major nor'easters.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Maine home?
faqSpecific.a: Average Maine home is about 1,600 sq ft. A 20 kW unit covers whole-house. Homes with electric heat (growing trend) need 22-24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: Can I use natural gas in Maine?
faqSpecific.a: Only in the Portland metro area. Most of Maine runs on propane or heating oil. Propane generators require a tank (250-500 gallon) which adds $500-$1,000 to installation.

faqSpecific.q: How reliable is CMP?
faqSpecific.a: Central Maine Power has been ranked among the worst utilities in New England for reliability. Multi-day outages after winter storms are common.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Maine?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most municipalities. Portland and surrounding communities are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need a larger propane tank in Maine?
faqSpecific.a: 500-gallon minimum is recommended. A 22 kW generator running at half load uses about 2 gallons/hour. A 500-gallon tank gives you 10+ days of backup.

fuelReason: PSE&G and NJ Natural Gas provide widespread service. Most suburban homes have natural gas.
recentEvent: Superstorm Sandy (2012) left 2.7 million NJ customers without power, some for over two weeks. Remnants of Ida (2021) caused catastrophic flooding across central NJ.
localCode: NJ Uniform Construction Code. Municipal permits required. Some towns require fire department approval for fuel storage. Shore communities have wind-speed and flood zone requirements.
costExplanation: Above-average costs due to high labor rates, complex municipal permitting, and strict building codes. The Jersey Shore and North Jersey are the most expensive.
bestTimeToBuy: December through March. Hurricane season runs June through November.
description: New Jersey was ground zero for Superstorm Sandy, which reshaped how the state thinks about power resilience. Generator installations surged after Sandy and again after Ida's flooding in 2021. High labor costs and complex permitting make NJ one of the more expensive states for installation.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a New Jersey home?
faqSpecific.a: Average NJ home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 22 kW unit is standard for whole-house. Larger shore homes may need 24-26 kW.

faqSpecific.q: Why is NJ so expensive for generators?
faqSpecific.a: Labor rates 25-35% above national average, complex municipal permitting, and strict building codes. A 22 kW install that costs $10,000 in the Midwest costs $14,000+ here.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in New Jersey?
faqSpecific.a: 2-4 weeks. Each municipality has its own process. Shore towns can take 4-6 weeks during peak season.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need flood-rated mounting at the Jersey Shore?
faqSpecific.a: In flood zones (most shore communities), yes. Your generator must be mounted above the FEMA base flood elevation. Adds $400-$800.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in New Jersey?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas for most NJ homes. Propane in the Pine Barrens, rural Sussex County, and some shore communities.

fuelReason: Nicor Gas and Ameren serve most of the state. Chicago suburbs have extensive natural gas infrastructure.
recentEvent: The August 2020 derecho ripped across Illinois with 100+ mph winds, leaving hundreds of thousands without power for days.
localCode: Illinois follows the NEC. Chicago has its own electrical code, which is stricter and requires city-licensed electricians. Suburban Cook County follows the county code.
costExplanation: Near the national average overall. Chicago and the North Shore are above average due to union labor rates and city code complexity. Downstate Illinois is below average.
bestTimeToBuy: September through January. Severe storm season peaks April through August.
description: Illinois faces severe thunderstorms and tornadoes in spring and summer, and bitter winter storms from November through March. The Chicago metro's dense tree canopy and older grid infrastructure make power outages a recurring frustration for ComEd customers.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for an Illinois home?
faqSpecific.a: Average IL home is about 2,100 sq ft. A 22 kW unit covers whole-house. Homes with electric heat may need 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: Is Chicago more expensive for generator installation?
faqSpecific.a: Yes. Chicago requires city-licensed electricians and has its own electrical code. Expect 15-25% above suburban pricing.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Illinois?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks in most suburbs. Chicago proper can take 3-4 weeks due to the city's separate electrical code.

faqSpecific.q: Are tornadoes a real risk in Illinois?
faqSpecific.a: Illinois averages 50-60 tornadoes per year, ranking in the top 10 nationally. Central and southern Illinois are in the highest-risk zones.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Illinois?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Chicago, suburbs, and most metro areas. Propane in rural downstate Illinois.

fuelReason: Southern Connecticut Gas, Yankee Gas, and Eversource provide natural gas to most suburban areas. Northern CT and rural towns rely on propane.
recentEvent: Tropical Storm Isaias (2020) knocked out power to over 700,000 Eversource customers in Connecticut, with some areas waiting 8+ days for restoration.
localCode: Connecticut State Building Code. Each municipality issues its own permits. Coastal zone (Fairfield County) has wind and flood requirements.
costExplanation: Above-average costs due to New England labor rates, complex town-level permitting, and Eversource's high electricity prices which increase generator ROI.
bestTimeToBuy: January through April. Nor'easter season peaks October through March.
description: Connecticut homeowners pay some of the highest electricity rates in the nation, making generator economics more favorable. Eversource, the primary utility, has faced intense criticism after repeated multi-day outages from storms. Generator installations surged after Isaias in 2020.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Connecticut home?
faqSpecific.a: Average CT home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 22 kW covers whole-house. Fairfield County homes tend to be larger and may need 24-26 kW.

faqSpecific.q: How reliable is Eversource?
faqSpecific.a: Eversource has been under fire for slow storm restoration. After Isaias (2020), some towns went 8+ days without power. The state legislature has investigated their performance.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Connecticut?
faqSpecific.a: 2-3 weeks in most towns. Each municipality handles its own permits — some are faster than others.

faqSpecific.q: Why are Connecticut generators worth the investment?
faqSpecific.a: CT has some of the highest electricity rates in the nation (~30¢/kWh). Combined with frequent outages, the ROI on a standby generator is faster here than in most states.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Connecticut?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Fairfield, New Haven, and Hartford metro areas. Propane in Litchfield Hills, eastern CT, and rural towns.

fuelReason: BGE (Baltimore Gas & Electric) and Washington Gas provide natural gas to most of the state's suburban areas.
recentEvent: The June 2024 derecho-type storms caused widespread outages across the Baltimore-DC corridor, leaving hundreds of thousands without power.
localCode: Maryland follows the IRC. Montgomery and Prince George's counties have additional code requirements. Eastern Shore communities have flood and wind requirements.
costExplanation: Above-average costs driven by DC metro labor rates in the western suburbs. Baltimore and the Eastern Shore are closer to average.
bestTimeToBuy: December through March. Storm season peaks May through October.
description: Maryland straddles multiple weather risk zones: hurricanes and coastal flooding on the Eastern Shore, severe thunderstorms in central Maryland, and ice storms in the western mountains. The DC metro suburbs face higher installation costs but also have excellent installer availability.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Maryland home?
faqSpecific.a: Average MD home is about 2,100 sq ft. A 22 kW is standard. Montgomery County homes (often 3,000+ sq ft) may need 24-26 kW.

faqSpecific.q: Is the DC metro area more expensive?
faqSpecific.a: Yes. Montgomery, Prince George's, Howard, and Anne Arundel counties run 15-25% above the state average due to DC metro labor rates.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Maryland?
faqSpecific.a: 1-3 weeks. Montgomery County has a thorough process. Baltimore city can be slower than surrounding counties.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need flood-rated mounting on the Eastern Shore?
faqSpecific.a: In FEMA flood zones along the Chesapeake Bay and Atlantic coast, yes. Elevated mounting adds $300-$600.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Maryland?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Baltimore, DC suburbs, and Annapolis. Propane on the Eastern Shore, western MD mountains, and rural areas.

fuelReason: National Grid and Eversource Gas provide natural gas to most of eastern and central Massachusetts. Cape Cod and western MA often use propane.
recentEvent: The October 2024 bomb cyclone brought hurricane-force winds to eastern Massachusetts, leaving over 500,000 customers without power for up to 5 days.
localCode: Massachusetts State Building Code (based on the IBC/IRC). Municipal permits required. Coastal communities have wind and flood zone requirements. Some towns restrict generator noise levels.
costExplanation: Among the most expensive northeastern states for installation. High labor rates, complex permitting, and strict building codes drive costs 30% above the national average.
bestTimeToBuy: January through April. Nor'easter season peaks October through March.
description: Massachusetts faces relentless nor'easters and coastal storms that regularly knock out power for days. The state's high electricity prices and long winters make generator ownership increasingly common, especially in suburban communities where tree-related outages are frequent.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Massachusetts home?
faqSpecific.a: Average MA home is about 1,800 sq ft. A 20-22 kW covers whole-house. Larger colonial homes in the suburbs may need 24 kW.

faqSpecific.q: Why is Massachusetts so expensive for generators?
faqSpecific.a: New England labor rates, complex municipal permitting, and strict building codes. A 22 kW install that costs $10,000 in Ohio costs $14,000+ here.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Massachusetts?
faqSpecific.a: 2-4 weeks in most towns. Newton, Wellesley, and Cape Cod towns tend to be the slowest.

faqSpecific.q: Do I need a generator on Cape Cod?
faqSpecific.a: Cape Cod loses power more frequently than the mainland due to exposure to nor'easters and limited transmission routes. A generator is strongly recommended.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Massachusetts?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Boston metro, Worcester, and Springfield. Propane on Cape Cod, the Islands, western MA, and rural communities.

fuelReason: Southwest Gas provides natural gas to the Phoenix and Tucson metros. Rural areas rely on propane.
recentEvent: The July 2023 monsoon season brought record haboobs and microbursts to the Phoenix metro, causing rolling outages during 115°F+ heat waves.
localCode: Arizona follows the IRC. Phoenix and Scottsdale have specific setback and noise requirements. Desert installations may need shade structures to prevent overheating.
costExplanation: Slightly above average due to heat-related installation requirements (concrete pads must cure differently, shade structures may be needed). Labor rates are moderate.
bestTimeToBuy: October through March. Monsoon season runs June through September, and extreme heat peaks in July-August.
description: Arizona's risk isn't just storms — it's extreme heat. When power goes out during a 115°F Phoenix summer day, it becomes a life-threatening emergency within hours. Monsoon microbursts, haboobs, and grid strain during peak cooling demand make backup power essential for Arizona homeowners.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for an Arizona home?
faqSpecific.a: Average AZ home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 22-24 kW unit is recommended because Arizona AC systems run harder than in any other state. Don't skimp on size here.

faqSpecific.q: Can my generator handle Arizona's extreme heat?
faqSpecific.a: Yes, but placement matters. Your installer should position the unit on the north side of the house if possible, and a shade structure may be recommended to prevent overheating in 115°F+ conditions.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Arizona?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks. Phoenix and Scottsdale have straightforward processes.

faqSpecific.q: Why is a generator critical in Arizona?
faqSpecific.a: A power outage during a Phoenix heat wave is a medical emergency. Indoor temps can exceed 100°F within 2-3 hours without AC. Backup power isn't a convenience — it's survival.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Arizona?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in Phoenix and Tucson metros via Southwest Gas. Propane in rural areas, mountain communities, and outlying areas.

fuelReason: Xcel Energy provides natural gas to most of the Front Range. Mountain communities often use propane.
recentEvent: The Marshall Fire (2021) was the most destructive wildfire in Colorado history, destroying 1,000+ homes in Boulder County and causing widespread power shutoffs.
localCode: Colorado follows the IRC. Wildfire interface zones have additional setback requirements. Mountain communities may have altitude-related derating considerations for generators.
costExplanation: Near the national average along the Front Range. Mountain installations cost more due to logistics, altitude derating, and limited installer availability.
bestTimeToBuy: May through August. Winter storms peak October through April. Wildfire season peaks June through September.
description: Colorado faces a dual threat: winter storms that can dump feet of snow on the Front Range, and increasingly destructive wildfires in the foothills and mountains. The Marshall Fire showed that even suburban communities near Boulder and Denver aren't immune to wildfire-related power loss.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Colorado home?
faqSpecific.a: Average CO home is about 2,100 sq ft. A 22 kW is standard. At altitude, generators may derate 3-4% per 1,000 feet above sea level — mountain homes should oversize.

faqSpecific.q: Does altitude affect generator performance?
faqSpecific.a: Yes. Generators lose about 3-4% power output per 1,000 feet of elevation. Denver (5,280 ft) loses ~15-20%. A 22 kW unit effectively produces ~18 kW. Mountain homes at 8,000+ ft should consider 24-26 kW units.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Colorado?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks on the Front Range. Mountain communities can take 2-4 weeks, especially in wildfire interface zones.

faqSpecific.q: Is wildfire a generator concern?
faqSpecific.a: In the wildfire-urban interface (WUI), generators must meet setback requirements from structures and vegetation. Your installer handles compliance.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Colorado?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas along the Front Range (Denver, Boulder, Colorado Springs). Propane in mountain communities, ski towns, and rural eastern plains.

fuelReason: Spire and Ameren provide natural gas to most of the state. Kansas City and St. Louis metros have extensive gas infrastructure.
recentEvent: The May 2019 EF3 tornado struck Jefferson City (the state capital), and the Joplin EF5 tornado (2011) remains one of the deadliest in US history.
localCode: Missouri follows the IRC. St. Louis and Kansas City have additional municipal code requirements. Standard electrical permit required.
costExplanation: Below-average costs due to Midwest labor rates, simple permitting, and cheap natural gas. St. Louis and Kansas City are slightly above rural Missouri.
bestTimeToBuy: September through January. Tornado and severe storm season peaks March through June.
description: Missouri sits at the crossroads of tornado alley and the ice storm belt. The state sees more tornadoes than most people realize, and the January 2009 ice storm left over 1 million homes without power for up to two weeks in the Ozarks region.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for a Missouri home?
faqSpecific.a: Average MO home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 20-22 kW unit covers whole-house. Most Missouri homes have gas heat.

faqSpecific.q: Is Missouri really tornado-prone?
faqSpecific.a: Very. Missouri averages 30-45 tornadoes per year. The Joplin EF5 (2011) killed 158 people and was one of the deadliest US tornadoes in decades.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Missouri?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks statewide. St. Louis and Kansas City are efficient.

faqSpecific.q: Are ice storms a concern in Missouri?
faqSpecific.a: Major concern. The January 2009 ice storm left 1 million without power for up to two weeks. The Ozarks are especially vulnerable.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Missouri?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas in KC, St. Louis, Springfield, and Columbia metros. Propane in the Ozarks, Bootheel, and rural areas.

fuelReason: CenterPoint Energy and Vectren provide natural gas to most Indiana homes. Cheap and abundant throughout the state.
recentEvent: The June 2023 derecho hit central Indiana with 90+ mph winds, knocking out power to hundreds of thousands across the Indianapolis metro.
localCode: Indiana follows the IRC. Standard residential electrical permit required. No unusual statewide requirements.
costExplanation: Among the cheapest states for installation. Low Midwest labor rates, simple permitting, and abundant natural gas keep costs well below the national average.
bestTimeToBuy: August through November. Tornado season peaks March through June.
description: Indiana sits in the heart of tornado alley's eastern extension, with severe thunderstorms, derechos, and winter ice storms adding to the risk. Low installation costs make standby generators accessible to Hoosier homeowners at prices well below the national average.

faqSpecific.q: What size generator for an Indiana home?
faqSpecific.a: Average Indiana home is about 2,000 sq ft. A 20-22 kW handles whole-house. Nearly all Indiana homes have gas heat.

faqSpecific.q: How affordable are generators in Indiana?
faqSpecific.a: Indiana is one of the cheapest states for installation. A whole-house 22 kW system starts around $7,500 — significantly below the national average.

faqSpecific.q: How long does permitting take in Indiana?
faqSpecific.a: 1-2 weeks statewide. Indianapolis (Marion County) is efficient.

faqSpecific.q: Are tornadoes common in Indiana?
faqSpecific.a: Indiana averages 20-30 tornadoes per year. The state sits at the intersection of warm Gulf air and cold northern fronts.

faqSpecific.q: Natural gas or propane in Indiana?
faqSpecific.a: Natural gas for the vast majority of Indiana homes. Propane only in the most rural areas of southern Indiana.
